item.title: React 与信号：10 年后 | React vs Signals: 10 Years Later
item.description:
旧的温斯顿丘吉尔语录如何？

那些不从历史中吸取教训的人注定要重蹈覆辙

尽管可能会添加更具讽刺意味的附录。

研究历史的人注定要袖手旁观，而其他人都在重复它。

在过去的几周里，我们看到了围绕细粒度反应性（被称为信号）在整个前端世界的复兴所带来的兴奋的高潮。

有关 JavaScript 中信号的历史，请查看我的文章：

事实上，信号从未消失。 它们作为第三方库或隐藏在框架的普通对象 API 后面默默无闻地存在了好几年。 尽管 React 和 Virtual DOM 出现的常见言论谴责这些模式不可预测且危险。 他们没有错。

很容易忘记，但关于信号的争论与我们 10 年前关于双向数据绑定与单向数据流的争论是一样的。 信号是可变状态！ 它们是具有新名称的绑定。 当更新流不可预测时，UI 作为状态函数的简单性就会丢失。

但这不仅仅是一场长达 10 年的辩论。 所以我想谈谈这些年来事情发生了怎样的变化，并提供SolidJS作为陪衬。

对某些人来说可能是这样。 但我也邀请考虑我们中的一些人之前已经经历过这种情况，甚至*开始*我们的职业生涯与设置/渲染分离。 对我们来说，React 是“修复”。

此次对话的核心是理解 React 是什么。 到目前为止，我对这个主题的解释之一来自 Dan Abramov 最早的一篇文章You're Missing the Point of React ，他在其中陈述 React 的真正优势是：

组合、单向数据流、免于 DSL、显式突变和静态心智模型。

甚至多年后，围绕 React 的思想领袖们提出了他们修复前端的想法。

但假设，如果他们不这样做呢？ 如果有其他方法可以解决当天的问题而不涉及如此激烈的重新调整，那会怎样？

可靠的选择

它甚至分享了组合、单向数据流和显式突变等理念，这些理念让 React 感觉像是 UI 开发的简单答案。 它的不同之处在于反应性世界之外的一切都是效果。 这几乎是 React 的对立面，React 将您所做的一切都视为纯粹的（就像没有副作用一样）。

当我想在没有任何模板的情况下使用 Signals 创建一个计数器按钮时，我会这样做：

 function MyCounter() { const [count, setCount] = createSignal(); const myButton = document.createElement("button"); myButton.onclick = () => setCount(count() + 1); // update the text initially and whenever count changes createEffect(() => { myButton.textContent = count(); }); return myButton; }

我会调用我的函数并返回一个按钮。 如果我需要另一个按钮，我会再做一次。 这是非常设置和忘记的。 我创建了一个 DOM 元素并设置了一些事件侦听器。 就像 DOM 本身一样，我不需要为我的按钮调用任何东西来更新。 它是独立的。 如果我想要一种更符合人体工学的写作方式，我会使用 JSX。

 function MyCounter() { const [count, setCount] = createSignal(); return <button onClick={() => setCount(count() + 1)}> {count()} </button> }

信号与过去不同。 他们的执行是无故障的。 它们是推/拉混合体，可以对预定的工作流程（如 Suspense 或并发渲染）进行建模。 并通过自动处理减轻泄漏的观察者模式。 多年来，他们不仅在更新方面而且在创作方面一直处于领先地位。

不变性

到目前为止，一切都很好？ 好吧，也许不是：

我发现很难相信 (a) 很容易添加仅 init 渲染逻辑和 (b) 使其响应式需要重构的方法，也可以组合。 这是我们*在* React 之前拥有的以及 React 解决的问题。

显然，这是 React 解决的问题。 他们具体解决了什么问题？

暂时搁置 Dan 的具体担忧，归结为不变性。 但不是以最直接的方式。 信号本身是不可变的。 您不能修改他们的内容并期望他们做出反应。

 const [list, setList] = createSignal([]); createEffect(() => console.log(JSON.stringify(list()))); list().push("Doesn't trigger"); setList(() => [...list(), "Does trigger"]);

即使对于使用.value的 Vue、Preact 或 Qwik 的变体，您也是在替换值而不是通过赋值改变它。 那么信号是“可变状态”是什么意思呢？

具有细粒度事件驱动架构的好处是可以进行隔离更新。 换句话说，变异。 相比之下，React 的纯渲染模型抽象出底层的可变世界，每次运行都重新创建其虚拟表示。

如果数据接口是显式的、副作用得到管理并且执行定义明确，那么在查看两个驱动状态更新的声明性库时，这种区别有多重要？

单向流

我不喜欢双向绑定。 单向流是一个非常好的东西。 我经历过这些推文中提到的相同事情。 您可能已经注意到 Solid 在其原语中采用了读/写隔离。 这甚至适用于其嵌套的反应式代理。

如果你创建一个反应原语，你会得到一个只读接口和一个写接口。 关于这一点的观点在 Solid 的设计中根深蒂固，以至于社区成员喜欢嘲笑我，滥用 getter 和 setter 来伪造可变性。

我想对 Solid 的设计做的重要事情之一是保持思维的局部性。 父母是否使用信号并不重要。 您根据需要创作。 如果需要，请将每个prop视为反应式，并在需要时访问它。 不需要全局思维。 无需担心可重构性。

我们通过建议在编写 props 时访问 Signal 的值而不是将其传递下去来强化这一点。 让您的组件期望值而不是信号。 如果 Solid 可能具有反应性，则通过将它们包装在 getter 中来保留反应性。

 <Greeting name={name()} /> // becomes Greeting({ get name() { return name() }) <Greeting name={"John"} /> // becomes Greeting({ name: "John" })

它怎么知道的？ 一个简单的启发式。 如果表达式包含函数调用或属性访问，它会将其包装起来。 因此，任何函数调用或属性访问（可能是 getter 或代理）都可能是反应式的，因此我们将其包装起来。

积极的是，无论您如何使用Greeting ，您都可以以相同的方式访问该属性： props.name 。 没有不必要的isSignal检查或过度包装来将事物变成信号。 作为一个价值，没有突变的期望。 道具是只读的，数据以一种方式流动。

选择加入与选择退出

哪种方式，框架作者

这可能是讨论的重点。 有很多方法可以解决这个问题。 出于开发人员体验的原因，大多数图书馆都选择了反应性，因为自动依赖性跟踪意味着无需担心丢失更新。

对于 React 开发人员来说，这并不难想象。 没有依赖数组的图片挂钩。 同样，您在使用 React 服务器组件时选择加入客户端组件（ use client ）。 多年来，还有其他解决方案通过编译自动执行此操作，但有时要明确说明一些事情。

这通常不是一个单一的决定。 在任何框架中，您都可以选择加入和退出。 实际上，所有的框架可能更像是这样的：

哪种方式，框架作者 https://t.co/ZWFlX1XaRI

框架的理想无可非议，但现实并非如此明确。

这让我想到了这个例子：

对于信号，您必须考虑如何传播每个单独的值。 例如，在 Solid 中，这些组件做不同的事情！ 考虑每个单独的道具是否可以改变需要全局推理而不是局部推理。

从 Solid 的角度来看，这是两个截然不同的函数，因为 Solid 的 JSX 处理以及它们只运行一次的事实。 一旦您意识到这一点，就不会模棱两可并且很容易避免。 甚至还有一个 lint 规则。

这就像期待这些是一样的：

 const value = Date.now(); function getTime1() { return value; } function getTime2() { return Date.now(); }

移动表达式不会改变Date.now()的作用，但提升会改变函数的行为。

也许它不太理想，但这种心智模式并非没有它自己的好处：

另外，您还可以获取 LSP 引用以进行更改。

只是看起来....最理性

这真的可以“修复”吗？

这是合乎逻辑的后续行动。 这在很大程度上是一个语言问题。 编译器面临的挑战是更难解释边缘情况并理解出现问题时会发生什么。 这在很大程度上是 React 或 Solid 在历史上一直非常谨慎地保持清晰界限的原因。

自从首次引入 Solid 以来，我们就有人探索不同的编译方式，因为作为基元的信号具有很强的适应性和高性能。

两个系统都有规定。 那是因为如果你这样做，你可以观察到抽象泄漏，如果它们曾经优化过引擎盖下的东西。 包括可能不会重新运行组件的某些部分。

最终结果几乎是一样的。

最后的想法

所有这一切中最奇怪的部分是 React 团队在查看 Reactivity 时并不觉得他们在照镜子。 通过添加 Hooks，他们牺牲了部分重新渲染的纯度，以获得接近 Signals 的模型。 通过添加一个编译器来移除与记忆有关的钩子，他们完成了这个故事。

现在应该明白了，这些都是独立开发的。 或者至少没有给出任何确认，考虑到老卫士的观点，这并不奇怪：

抛弃 React 编程模型并采用信号之类的东西（或者更糟，信号加 dsl）是一个巨大的倒退，大多数人还没有将其内化

幸运的是，今天的 React 也不是 10 年前的 React。

他们之所以这样做，是因为坚信自己是混乱海洋中独特的理性声音。 由于他们的出色工作，我们取得了今天的成就，无数人从他们的教训中吸取了教训。

我们喜欢信号的性能特性。 我们不喜欢您必须编写代码才能获得它。

时代变了。 被“固定”掉的范式重新出现几乎是合适的。 它关闭了循环。 完成故事。 当所有的噪音和部落主义都消失后，剩下的就是良性竞争推动网络向前发展的故事。

断章取义拼凑一个故事可以说是不礼貌的。 时代在前进，观点在改变。 但这是 React 的思想领导层长期以来强烈持有的观点。 他们从一开始就大声疾呼。 我能够毫不费力地从过去几天的对话中获取所有这些引述。

有关 React 早期历史的更多信息，请观看：



原文:

How does the old Winston Churchill quote go?

Those who fail to learn from history are doomed to repeat it

Although a more ironic addendum might add.

Those who study history are doomed to stand by while everyone else repeats it.

In the past few weeks, we've seen the culmination of a build of excitement around the revival of fine-grained reactivity, being referred to as Signals, across the front-end world.

For a history of Signals in JavaScript check out my article:

The truth is Signals never went away. They lived several years in obscurity as third-party libraries or hidden behind frameworks' plain object APIs. Even though the common rhetoric that came in with React and the Virtual DOM condemned the patterns as unpredictable and dangerous. And they weren't wrong.

Easy to forget, but the debate about signals is the same one we had about 2-way data binding vs unidirectional data flow 10 years ago. Signals are mutable state! They’re bindings with a new name. The simplicity of UI as a function of state is lost when updates flow unpredictably.

But there is more to it than a 10 year old debate. So I want to talk about how things have changed over the years and offer SolidJS as a foil.

"Fixing" Front-end

@thdxr there’s an assumption that people with React brain are unfamiliar with other approaches. that’s probably true for some. but i also invite to consider that some of us have been through this before and even *started* our careers with setup/render split. to us, React was the “fix”.

At the core of this conversation is understanding what React is. React is not its Virtual DOM. React is not JSX. To this date one of my explanations on the subject came from one of Dan Abramov's earliest articles You're Missing the Point of React, where he state React's true strengths are:

composition, unidirectional data flow, freedom from DSLs, explicit mutation, and static mental model.

React has a very powerful set of principles that guide it that are more important than any implementation detail. And even years later there is this notion from the thought leaders around React that they fixed front-end.

But hypothetically, what if they didn't? What if there were other ways to address the problems of the day that didn't involve such drastic re-alignment?

A Solid Alternative

The concept behind Solid is equally simple. It even shares ideas like composition, unidirectional data flow, and explicit mutation that made React feel like a simple answer to UI development. Where it differs is outside the world of reactivity everything is an Effect. It's almost the antithesis of React which treats everything you do as being pure (as in having no side effects).

When I think of creating a Counter button with Signals without any templating I'd do this:

function MyCounter() { const [count, setCount] = createSignal(); const myButton = document.createElement("button"); myButton.onclick = () => setCount(count() + 1); // update the text initially and whenever count changes createEffect(() => { myButton.textContent = count(); }); return myButton;
}

I'd call my function and get a button back. If I need another button I'd do it again. This is very much set and forget. I created a DOM element and set up some event listeners. Like the DOM itself, I don't need to call anything for my button to update. It is independent. If I want a more ergonomic way of writing I use JSX.

function MyCounter() { const [count, setCount] = createSignal(); return <button onClick={() => setCount(count() + 1)}> {count()} </button>
}

Signals are not the same Signals as yesteryear. Their execution is glitch-free. They are push/pull hybrids that can model scheduled workflows like Suspense or Concurrent Rendering. And mitigate the leaky observer pattern with automated disposal. They have been leading benchmarks for several years not only for updates but for creation.

Immutability

So far so good? Well maybe not:

@thdxr it’s one of those “does it scale” things. i am finding it difficult to believe that the approach where (a) it’s easy to add init-only rendering logic and (b) making it reactive requires refactorings, composes just as well. it’s what we had *before* React and what React solved.

Apparently, this is something React solves. What did they solve exactly?

Putting Dan's specific concerns aside for a moment, it comes down to immutability. But not in the most direct way. Signals themselves are immutable. You can't modify their content and expect them to react.

const [list, setList] = createSignal([]); createEffect(() => console.log(JSON.stringify(list()))); list().push("Doesn't trigger"); setList(() => [...list(), "Does trigger"]);

Even with variants from Vue, Preact, or Qwik that use .value you are replacing the value not mutating it by assignment. So what does it mean that Signals are "mutable state"?

The benefit of having a granular event-driven architecture is to do isolated updates. In other words, mutate. In contrast, React's pure render model abstracts away the underlying mutable world re-creating its virtual representation with each run.

How important is this distinction when looking at two declarative libraries that drive updates off state if the data interfaces are explicit, side effects managed, and the execution well-defined?

Unidirectional Flow

I am not a fan of 2-way binding. Unidirectional Flow is a really good thing. I lived through the same things that are referenced in these tweets. You may have noticed Solid employs read/write segregation in its primitives. This is even true of its nested reactive proxies.

If you create a reactive primitive you get a read-only interface and a write interface. The opinion on this is so ingrained into Solid's design that members of the community like to troll me, abusing getters and setters to fake mutability.

One of the important things I wanted to do with Solid's design was to keep the locality of thinking. All the work in Solid is done where the effects are which is where we insert into the DOM. It doesn't matter if the parent uses a Signal or not. You author to your need. Treat every prop as reactive if you need it to be and access it where you need it. There is no global thinking necessary. No concerns with refactorability.

We re-enforce this by recommending when writing props you access the Signal's value rather than pass it down. Have your components expect values rather than Signals. Solid preserves reactivity by wrapping these in getters if it could be reactive.

<Greeting name={name()} />

// becomes
Greeting({ get name() { return name() }) <Greeting name={"John"} />

// becomes
Greeting({ name: "John" })

How does it know? A simple heuristic. If the expression contains a function call or property access, it wraps it. Reactive values in JavaScript have to be function calls so that we can track the reads. So any function call or property access, which could be a getter or proxy, could be reactive so we wrap it.

The positive is that for Greeting regardless of how you are consumed you access the property the same way: props.name. There is no isSignal check or overwrapping unnecessarily to make things into Signals. props.name is always a string. And being a value there is no expectation of mutation. Props are read-only and data flows one way.

Opt-In vs Opt-Out

which way, framework author

This might be the crux of the discussion. There are a lot of ways to approach this. Most libraries have chosen reactivity for Developer Experience reasons because automatic dependency tracking means not needing to worry about missing updates.

It isn't hard to imagine for a React developer. Picture Hooks without the dependency arrays. The existence of the dependency arrays in Hooks suggests React can miss updates. Similarly, you opt into client components (use client) when using React Server Components. There are other solutions that have been automating this via compilation for years, but at times there is something to be said about being explicit.

It isn't generally a singular decision. You have things you opt into and things you opt out of in any framework. In reality, all frameworks are probably more like this:

which way, framework author https://t.co/ZWFlX1XaRI

A frameworks ideals can be beyond reproach but the reality is not so clear cut.

This brings me to this example:

With signals, you have to think about how each individual value is propagated. For example, in Solid, these components do different things! Thinking about whether each individual prop can change or not requires global reasoning rather than local.

These are 2 very different functions from Solid's perspective, because of Solid's JSX handling and the fact they only run once. This is not ambiguous and easily avoided once you are aware. And there is even a lint rule for that.

It's like expecting these to be the same:

const value = Date.now();
function getTime1() { return value;
} function getTime2() { return Date.now();
}

Moving the expression doesn't change what Date.now() does but hoisting changes the function's behavior.

Maybe it is less than ideal, but it isn't like this mental model isn't without its own benefits:

@acdlite its funny, the first time i ever used signals wasn't for perf, it was for understanding where change happens easier. plus you get LSP references to change.

just seems.... most rational

Can this be "Fixed" for real?

That's the logical follow-up. This is very much a language problem. What does fixed even look like? The challenge with compilers is that it is harder to account for edge cases and understand what happens when things go wrong. It is largely the reason historically React or Solid has been pretty careful about keeping clear boundaries.

Since the first introduction of Solid we've had people exploring different compilation because Signals as primitives are very adaptable and very performant.

In 2021, I took a stab at it.

React team also announced they were looking at this too.

There are rules being imposed on both systems. React wants you to remember not to do impure things in your function bodies. That is because if you do you can observe abstraction leaks if they were ever to optimize what is under the hood. Including potentially not re-running parts of your component.

Solid is already optimized without the need for the compiler or extra wrappers like React.memo, useCallback, useRef, but like React could benefit from some more streamlined ergonomics like not having to worry about indicating where Signals are read.

The end result is almost the same.

Final Thoughts

The oddest part of all of this is that the React team when looking at Reactivity doesn't feel like they are looking in the mirror. By adding Hooks they sacrificed part of their re-render purity for a model approaching that of Signals. And that by adding a compiler to remove the hooks concerned with memoization they complete that story.

Now it should be understood these were developed independently. Or atleast no acknowledgement was ever given which is not surprising given the sentiment of the old guard:

@acdlite I think you guys should be way more aggressive in pushing back against this stuff. Throwing out the react programming model and adopting something like signals (or worse, signals plus a dsl) is a huge step backwards and most people haven’t internalized that

Fortunately React today is not the React of 10 years ago either.

React changed the front-end world by teaching us the important principles that should guide how to build UIs. They did so by strength of conviction being a unique voice of reason in a sea of chaos. We are where we are today due to their great work and countless have learned from their lessons.

@youyuxi @trueadm When @Huxpro introduced Forget at React Conf we focused on the auto-memoizing part, but the plan all along has involved compiling to a lower level reactive runtime. We like the perf characteristics of signals. We don't like the code you have to write to get it.

Times have changed. It is almost fitting that the paradigm that was "fixed" away would resurface. It closes the loop. Completes the story. And when all the noise and tribalism fade what remains is a tale of healthy competition driving the web forward.

It is arguably never polite to construct a narrative by sewing together quotes out of context. Time moves on, and perspectives change. But this is a long and strongly held sentiment from React's thought leadership. One they've been vocal about since the beginning. I was able to grab all these quotes over conversations from just the last couple of days with very little effort.

For more on React's early history watch:
item.author: ryansolid

item.title: 使用 OpenAI 嵌入的人工智能搜索 | AI powered search with OpenAI embeddings
item.description:
一些 AI 的最佳实现使用现有的知识基础，并通过提示使它们可搜索。 在浏览 Twitter 时，我在paul-graham-gpt上发了一条推文。

我嵌入了@paulg的所有文章。全部价值 605,870 个代币。

使用 OpenAI 的新模型在paul-graham-gpt.vercel.app上搜索并与他们聊天。

代码和数据集是 100% 开源的，任何人都可以使用。

在这30 天的 OpenAI 系列中，我正在研究 AI 项目及其代码，以帮助揭开任何开发人员如何构建 AI 生成项目的神秘面纱。

他还撰写了许多文章，为当前和未来的初创公司创始人提供了大量知识。

使用的所有代码和数据都是 100% 开源的。

数据集

数据集是一个 CSV 文件，包含所有使用的文本和嵌入。

在这里下载。

我建议您熟悉下面抓取和嵌入脚本中概述的数据的获取、清理和存储，但您可以随意跳过这些步骤，直接使用数据集。

怎么运行的

一个搜索界面。

  • 一个聊天界面。

    • 搜索

    搜索是使用OpenAI 嵌入( text-embedding-ada-002 ) 创建的。

    首先，我们遍历文章并为每个文本块生成嵌入。

    然后在应用程序中，我们获取用户的搜索查询，生成嵌入，并使用结果从书中找到最相似的段落。

    比较是在我们的向量数据库中使用余弦相似度完成的。

    我们的数据库是 Postgres…

    当仔细查看代码时，它使用了来自 OpenAI 的嵌入API。

    嵌入通常用于：
    这是我第一次了解嵌入，如果您阅读了我之前关于aicommits 的帖子，那使用了补全。 根据我对文档的阅读，嵌入在遍历现有数据和寻找相关性时很有用。 代码示例使用亚马逊食品评论作为文档中的示例。 您可能正在寻找有关调味品的评论，而您正在寻找的相关性是负面评论。 嵌入检查音调和收视率。

    乍一看这是我最好的解释，但请查看嵌入用例以获取更多上下文

    它是如何工作的？

    该项目的自述文件很好地解释了这些技术。 作者正在遍历所有文章并为每个文本块生成嵌入。 这是在generateEmbeddings函数中完成的。

    所有论文内容都存储在scripts/pg.json

     // scripts/embed.ts // this response is loop over using the essay content in the generateEmbeddings fnction const embeddingResponse = await openai.createEmbedding({ model: "text-embedding-ada-002", input: content }); ... // This is parsing the essays from the JSON (async () => { const book: PGJSON = JSON.parse(fs.readFileSync("scripts/pg.json", "utf8")); await generateEmbeddings(book.essays); })();

    然后他们将用户的搜索查询生成一个嵌入，并使用结果从文章中找到最相关的段落。

     // pages/api/search.ts const res = await fetch("https://api.openai.com/v1/embeddings", { headers: { "Content-Type": "application/JSON", Authorization: `Bearer ${apiKey}` }, method: "POST", body: JSON.stringify({ model: "text-embedding-ada-002", input }) }); ...

    比较是在我们的向量数据库中使用余弦相似度完成的。

     // pages/api/search.ts const { data: chunks, error } = await supabaseAdmin.RPC("pg_search", { query_embedding: embedding, similarity_threshold: 0.01, // cosine similarity match_count: matches });

    这是Supabase 上个月刚刚宣布的。

    结果按相似度得分排序并返回给用户。

    我喜欢浏览代码并了解它是如何工作的。 如果我需要更正某些内容，或者如果您对代码有一些了解，请发表评论。 感谢McKay与我们分享这一点，一定要关注他们并查看他们在 AI 方面的其他工作， Codewand AI 支持的工具可帮助您的团队更快地构建软件。

    此外，如果您有一个利用 OpenAI 的项目，请在评论中留下链接。 我很想看一看并将其包含在我的30 天 OpenAI 系列中。

    保持俏皮。

    图像是使用midjourney生成的



    原文:

    Some of the best implementations of AI use existing knowledge basis and make them searchable through prompts. While scrolling through Twitter, I a tweet on paul-graham-gpt.

    I embedded all of @paulg’s essays.All 605,870 tokens worth.

    Use OpenAI’s new model to search & chat with them at paul-graham-gpt.vercel.app.

    Code & dataset is 100% open-source for anyone to use.

    GitHub: github.com/mckaywrigley/p…

    In this 30 days of OpenAI series, I am looking at AI projects and their code to help demystify how any dev can build AI-generated projects.

    Paul Graham is best known for his work on the programming language Lisp and for cofounding the influential startup accelerator and seed capital firm Y Combinator. He also writes many essays that provide a lot of knowledge for current and future startup founders.

    Mckay Wrigley built a tool called paul-graham-gpt, you can use it live here, to navigate all of PaulG's essays, and I am excited to jump in and take a look on how he did this.

    AI-powered search and chat for Paul Graham's essays.

    All code & data used is 100% open-source.

    Dataset

    The dataset is a CSV file containing all text & embeddings used.

    Download it here.

    I recommend getting familiar with fetching, cleaning, and storing data as outlined in the scraping and embedding scripts below, but feel free to skip those steps and just use the dataset.

    How It Works

    Paul Graham GPT provides 2 things:

    1. A search interface.
    2. A chat interface.

    Search

    Search was created with OpenAI Embeddings (text-embedding-ada-002).

    First, we loop over the essays and generate embeddings for each chunk of text.

    Then in the app we take the user's search query, generate an embedding, and use the result to find the most similar passages from the book.

    The comparison is done using cosine similarity across our database of vectors.

    Our database is a Postgres…

    How was mckaywrigley/paul-graham-gpt made?

    paul-graham-gpt is described as AI search & chat for all of Paul Graham’s essays. When looking closer at the code, it uses the embeddings API from OpenAI.

    _OpenAI’s text embeddings measure the relatedness of text strings. Embeddings are commonly used for:
    _

    This is my first look at embeddings, and if you read in my previous post on aicommits, that used completions. Based on my reading in the docs, embeddings are useful when traversing existing data and looking for relevancy. The code samples use Amazon food reviews as the example in the docs. You might be looking for reviews on condiments, and the relevance you are looking for is negative reviews. The embeddings check tone along with ratings.

    That is my best explanation after a first look, but check out the embeddings use cases for more context

    How does it work?

    The project's README does a great job explaining the techniques. The author is looping over all essays and generating embeddings for each text chunk. This is done in the generateEmbeddings function.

    All essay content is stored in scripts/pg.json

    // scripts/embed.ts // this response is loop over using the essay content in the generateEmbeddings fnction
const embeddingResponse = await openai.createEmbedding({ model: "text-embedding-ada-002", input: content
}); ... // This is parsing the essays from the JSON
(async () => { const book: PGJSON = JSON.parse(fs.readFileSync("scripts/pg.json", "utf8")); await generateEmbeddings(book.essays);
})();

    Then they take the user's search query to generate an embedding and use the result to find the most relevant passages from the essays.

    // pages/api/search.ts const res = await fetch("https://api.openai.com/v1/embeddings", { headers: { "Content-Type": "application/JSON", Authorization: `Bearer ${apiKey}` }, method: "POST", body: JSON.stringify({ model: "text-embedding-ada-002", input })
}); ...

    The comparison is done using cosine similarity across our database of vectors.

    // pages/api/search.ts const { data: chunks, error
} = await supabaseAdmin.RPC("pg_search", { query_embedding: embedding, similarity_threshold: 0.01, // cosine similarity match_count: matches
});

    The Postgres database has the pgvector extension hosted on Supabase. This was just announced recently by Supabase last month.

    Results are ranked by similarity score and returned to the user.

    I enjoyed walking through the code and learning how this works. If I need to correct something, or if you have some insight into the code, please comment. Thanks to McKay for sharing this with us, and be sure to give them a follow and check out their other work in AI, Codewand AI-powered tools to help your team build software faster.

    Also, if you have a project leveraging OpenAI, leave a link in the comments. I'd love to take a look and include it in my 30 days of OpenAI series.

    Stay saucy.

    image was generated using midjourney

因此，这里列出了作为React开发人员学习不会出错的 7 个库。

原文:

On your journey to becoming a well-rounded React developer, you would come across innumerable libraries, leaving you dazed and confused.

So, here is a list of 7 libraries that can't go wrong with learning as a React developer.

1. React Joyride

React Joyride is a library that helps you create walkthroughs and guided tours for your React apps.

It is an incredible tool to showcase new features to existing users & onboarding new users to your app.

Website: react-joyride.com/

NPM: www.npmjs.com/package/react-joyride

2. React PDF Renderer

React PDF Renderer is a library that helps you create PDF files using React components, thus making the tedious process of creating PDFs a walk in the park.

Website: react-pdf.org/

NPM: www.npmjs.com/package/@react-pdf/render…

3. React Beautiful DnD

React Beautiful DnD is a library that helps you create drag and drop interfaces in your React apps.

Website: react-beautiful-dnd.netlify.app/

NPM: www.npmjs.com/package/react-beautiful-d…

4. Material UI

This is a library that needs no introduction. Material UI is the largest component library for React. It is used by Spotify, Amazon, Netflix and countless other companies.

Website: material-ui.com/

NPM: www.npmjs.com/package/@mui/material

5. Swiper.js

Swiper.js, as the library name suggests, is a library that helps you create swipeable interfaces in your React apps.

Website: swiperjs.com/react

NPM: www.npmjs.com/package/swiper

6. React Query

React Query is a data management library which provides immense control of fetching, caching data, and error handling using simple hooks.

Website: tanstack.com/query/latest/

NPM: www.npmjs.com/package/@tanstack/react-q…

7. React Spring

React Spring is a library that helps you create eye-candy animations in your React apps, which ensures high user engagement!

Website: react-spring.io/

NPM: www.npmjs.com/package/react-spring

That's all folks! 🎉

Want to connect? Reach out to me on LinkedIn

Follow my blogs for bi-weekly new Tidbits on Dev

FAQ

These are a few commonly asked questions I get. So, I hope this FAQ section solves your issues.
item.author: ruppysuppy

item.title: 从笨学生到 PyTorch 贡献者：老师对我生活的影响⚡ | From a Dumb Student to a PyTorch Contributor: The Impact of Teachers on My Life⚡
item.description:
我只能让他们思考。
- Socrates

教师在塑造我们的生活、激励我们追逐梦想并指导我们完成学习过程中发挥着重要作用。 在这篇文章中，我将分享一个真实的故事，说明老师对我生活的影响，以及它如何促使我为一个流行的开源项目做出贡献。 我还将讨论我在 PyTorch 中实施研究算法时面临的挑战，以及创建成功的行业就绪拉取请求所需的奉献精神和辛勤工作。 最后，我将反思看到我的拉取请求被合并的喜悦，以及它可能对其他人产生的影响。 这篇文章强调了从错误中吸取教训、寻求社区的帮助和反馈以及认可激励我们的人的贡献的重要性。

为开源项目做贡献背后的灵感。

回到学校时，我还是一个机器人（哑巴），我在 JNP 学校 Anawal 的第 3 标准，我在那里度过了 8 年，这无疑是惊人的，这是我的计算机老师（Nitin 先生）和我之间的对话。

Nitin sir:- "How do computers work? Vivek stand up and answer the question"

Vivek( nervous):- "um... Umm...."

Vivek again "1. input 2. output...ummmm 3.....processing"

(The whole class laughed)

Someone from right beside me( whispering):- it's, 1. input 2. processing 3. output, DUMBO.

(Again the whole class gave a howl of laughter )

然后，即使我给出了错误的答案，我的先生又给我举了一个例子，说明面粉机的工作原理。 从我记事起，我就是一个好奇的孩子，但我知道正是在那一刻，我对计算机产生了热情。 我的先生又问了我同样的问题，这次我纠正了自己。

If you are planning for a year, sow rice; if you are planning for a decade, plant trees; if you are planning for a lifetime, educate people.
– Chinese Proverb

那件事发生 16 年后，我仍然以机器学习的形式做错了"1.input+3.output" -> "2.processing"-> 4.rules!!!!

就在几天前，在我的拉取请求被合并之前。 我的先生在路上遇到我，我摸了他的脚，我的脑海里开始回忆起那些记忆，我仍然没有偿还他教我的东西。

你可以从我上面的真实故事中学到三件事：

  1. 犯错误是学习过程的一部分。

就像机器学习一样，犯错是很重要的部分，完全没关系！ 老师在塑造我们的生活中扮演着重要的角色

即使在 16 年之后，我仍然记得这段珍贵的谈话。 特别是我所有阅读这篇文章的老师，我爱你。 我不认为我将永远能够创建这篇文章并在我的拉取请求如何合并到 Pytoch 中加上标题； 学习是一个终生的过程，输入+输出=规则

在 Nitin 先生之后，我遇到了许多老师认为这一旅程，在我的学校，我遇到的第一位拥有博士学位的老师是 KJ Goswami，据我记得他是在氮环方面进行研究的。 他之前向我介绍了研究这个词，我不知道“研究”这个词的存在，我想再说几位老师，比如 Kinjal Mistree、Vishwajit Bakrola、Ankit Kharwar，还有更多教我输入+输出 =规则。有趣的是我仍在逐步学习。他们鼓励并激励我突破自己的极限。

在 PyTorch 中实现研究算法的挑战。

现在让我们回到您如何为开源项目做出贡献的过程。

我个人的挑战是：-

  1. 我不知道如何阅读和实施研究论文。
  2. 如何编写行业就绪代码

我在实施过程中遇到的挑战。

第一件事就像任何新手一样，我不知道如何阅读任何研究论文。 所以我跳到 youtube 上搜索了一篇研究论文是如何写的以及如何实施它。 我花了 8 天时间阅读这篇论文，因为它有很多反向链接 Geoffrey Hinton 喜欢在论文每一页的底部添加难以想象的单词和模因（其中一些很荒谬）。 所以在 8 天之后，我学到了很多对我实现代码有帮助的主题。 我搜索了 GitHub，只有 4 到 5 个存储库在 TensorFlow/Keras 中实现了相同，在 PyTorch 中没有实现。 所以我决定在 pytorch/examples 存储库中创建一个问题，

幸运的是，我收到了 PyTorch 创始人 Soumith Chintala 的回复，他是 Facebook AI Research 的一名研究员，从事高性能深度学习。

所以如果我能把它分解成点，那就是：-

  1. 了解阅读论文需要时间，尤其是当您是新手时
  2. 首先阅读摘要和结论
  3. 如果可能的话，记下纸上使用的数学方程式（它可以是损失函数，任何转换技术，ext ... ext ..）
  4. 在纸上突出显示您没有得到的要点

提示：-（这很常见，如果您不明白任何句子的含义，请通过论文底部提供的反向链接。因为他们假设您已经具备这些论文的基础知识）

专业提示：-（在任何研究论文的末尾都有一个名为“未来作品”的部分，您可以尝试实施这些项目，这很适合您的简历。）

创建成功的拉取请求所需的奉献精神和辛勤工作。

当我的朋友们正在享受所谓的“运动周”时，我是那个坐在 E101 中和我的朋友 Jainaam 一起尝试并努力实现前向算法的整个代码的人。 我花了 8 天时间阅读这篇论文，并花了整整一个月的时间在 PyTorch 中创建我的实现，但我的代码仍然不是最优的。

从社区寻求帮助和反馈的重要性。

之后我再次在 GitHub 上搜索，有没有可用的代码，幸运的是我遇到了计算机科学领域的研究员 Mohammad Pezeshki。 他的代码是最佳的，所以我就我的问题和请求请求向他发送了一封电子邮件，幸运的是他给予了肯定的答复。

就像电影卡比尔辛格一样，你需要一个最好的朋友，而you are average of five people surrounded by you. 就我而言，jainaam 帮助我使用 rust 和 makefile 构建文档。

看到您的拉取请求被合并的喜悦及其对其他人的影响。

在与我的维护者交谈后，他提出了一些更改和解释，我再次纠正了我的错误并相应地更新了我的代码。 然后从两端进行审查，错误后错误，提交后提交和更正后的更正。

最后，在 2023 年 2 月 27 日，我收到一封电子邮件，说我的拉取请求已合并到 PyTorch 中。

蛋糕上的樱桃 我在评论的代码顶部添加了我父亲的名字。 我希望在接下来的 200-300 年里，有人会阅读修改和改进或使用我的代码进行实验。（Vivek V patel），我的代码可以在 PyTorch 的官方网站pytorch.org/examples/ （图像分类使用前向算法）

我想用一句话结束这个博客

the person who goes to the gym every single day, regardless of how they feel, will always beat the person who goes to the gym when they feel like going to the gym.
-Unknown

感谢您阅读到这里，您可以在这里订阅我的电子邮件时事通讯，它是免费的（别担心，我不会发送垃圾邮件🤫🤫） vivekpatel.beehiiv.com



原文:

`I cannot teach anybody anything; I can only make them think.
- Socrates

Teachers play an important role in shaping our lives, inspiring us to chase our dreams and guiding us through the learning process. In this post, I'll share a real-life story that illustrates the impact of teachers on my life and how it led me to contribute to an popular open-source project. I'll also discuss the challenges I faced in implementing a research algorithm in PyTorch and the dedication and hard work required to create a successful industry ready pull request. Finally, I'll reflect on the joy of seeing my pull request merged and the impact it can have on others. This post highlights the importance of learning from our mistakes, seeking help and feedback from the community, and recognizing the contributions of those who inspire us.

The inspiration behind contributing to open-source projects.

Back in school when I was a bot(dumb), I was in 3rd standard at JNP school Anawal where I spent my 8 years which were undoubtedly amazing, here is a conversation between my computer teacher(Nitin sir) and me.

Nitin sir:- "How do computers work? Vivek stand up and answer the question"

Vivek( nervous):- "um... Umm...."

Vivek again "1. input 2. output...ummmm 3.....processing"

(The whole class laughed)

Someone from right beside me( whispering):- it's, 1. input 2. processing 3. output, DUMBO.

(Again the whole class gave a howl of laughter )

Then even though I'd given the wrong answer, my sir again gave me an example of how flour mil works. I was a curious child from beginning as far as I can remember but I know that in that exact moment I developed a passion for computers. My sir again asked me the same question and this time, I corrected myself.

If you are planning for a year, sow rice; if you are planning for a decade, plant trees; if you are planning for a lifetime, educate people.
– Chinese Proverb

after 16 years of that incident, I'm still doing it wrong in the form of machine learning "1.input+3.output" -> "2.processing"-> 4.rules!!!!

just some days ago before my pull request got merged. my sir met me on the road I touched his feet, and my mind started recalling all those memories, it's still nothing for me to pay off what he taught me.

You can learn 3 things from my above real-life story:

  1. Making mistakes is a part of the learning process.

Just like Machine learning it's an important part to make mistakes and it is completely okay! ; ) either it's your model or you can't learn without doing mistakes.

  1. Teachers play an important role in shaping up our lives

even after 16 years, I'm still remembering this precious conversation. especially all my teachers reading this post I love you. I don't think I will be ever able to create this post and put a title on how my pull request got merged in Pytoch ; )

  1. Learning is a lifelong process and input+output = rules

After Nitin sir, I met many teachers thought this journey, in my school the first teacher I met who was holding a PhD was K.J Goswami, as far as I can recall he done his research in nitrogen rings. he introduced research word to me before that I didn't have a clue that the term research" exists, I would like to name few more teachers like Kinjal Mistree, Vishwajit Bakrola, Ankit Kharwar, and many more who taught me input+output = rules. and the fun part is I'm still learning incrementally. They encourage me and inspire me to push my limits.

The challenge of implementing a research algorithm in PyTorch.

Now let's back to the process that how you can contribute to an open-source project.

My personal challenges were:-

  1. I didn't know how to read and implement research paper.
  2. How to write industry ready code

Challenges I faced during implementation.

The first thing is just like any newbie I didn't know how to read any research paper. so I hopped on to youtube and searched how a research paper is written and how one can implement it. it took me 8 days to read the paper because it has many backlinks Geoffrey Hinton likes to add unimaginable words and memes(some of which were ridiculous) at the bottom of every page of the paper. so after 8 days, I learned many topics that helped me in the implementation of code. I've searched GitHub there are only 4 to 5 repositories that are implementing the same in TensorFlow/Keras no implementations in PyTorch. So I decided to create an issue in the pytorch/examples repository,

and luckily I got a reply from PyTorch founder Soumith Chintala is a Researcher at Facebook AI Research, where he works on high-performance deep learning.

So if I could break that into points it would be:-

  1. Understand that it will take time to read a paper especially when you are new
  2. Read abstract and conclusion in the first place
  3. If possible take notes and notedown math equations that are used in paper( it could be loss function, any transformation technique, ext...ext..)
  4. Highlight points in paper that you don't get

Tip:- (This is very common, If you don't get meaning of any sentence go through backlink provided at the bottom of the paper. Because they assumes that you have already have base knowledge of these papers)

Pro tip:- ( at the end of any research paper there is one section called 'future works', you can try to implement those projects and it would be nice fit for your resume.)

The dedication and hard work required to create a successful pull request.

When my friends are enjoying so-called "Sports Week ", I was the one who was sitting in E101 and trying and struggling to implement the whole code of the forward forward algorithm with my friend Jainaam. It took me 8 days to read the paper and a full month to create my implementation in PyTorch and still, my code was not optimal.

The importance of seeking help and feedback from the community.

After that I again searched on GitHub, that is there any code available luckily I came across Mohammad Pezeshki, Who is a researcher in the computer science field. his code was optimal so I sent an email to him about my issue and my pull request, luckily he responded affirmatively.

Just like movie Kabir Singh you need a best friend and you are average of five people surrounded by you. In my case jainaam helped me in building docs using rust and makefile.

The joy of seeing your pull request being merged and the impact it can have on others.

After some talk with my maintainer, he suggested some changes and explanations, I've again corrected my mistakes and updated my code accordingly. Then reviews after review, mistake after mistakes, commit after commits and corrections after correction from both ends.

Finally, on 27 Feb 2023, I received an email that my pull request is merged in PyTorch.

The cherry on top of the cake I've added my father's name at the top of the code in the comments. I hope that for the next upcoming 200-300 years, someone will read modify and improve or perform experiments with my code.(Vivek V patel), My code can be found at official PyTorch's Website pytorch.org/examples/(Image Classification Using Forward-Forward Algorithm)

I would Like to end this Blog with a quote

the person who goes to the gym every single day, regardless of how they feel, will always beat the person who goes to the gym when they feel like going to the gym.
-Unknown

Thanks for reading this far, you can sub to my email newsletter here and it's free(Don't worry I don't spam🤫🤫) vivekpatel.beehiiv.com
item.author: vivekcodes

item.title: 如何使用不同的方法在python中组合两个字典 | How to combine two dictionaries in python using different methods
item.description:
与 python 不同，字典中的列表值是使用键检索或存储的。 字典使用花括号 {} 和由 : 冒号分隔的键值对声明。

使用 python for 循环合并两个字典

mydict1 = { "car": 5, "bus": 3, "bike": 10 } mydict2 = { "cycle": 4, "truck": 8, "car": 6 } for key in mydict2: mydict1[key] = mydict2[key] print("mydict1:", mydict1) Output: mydict1: {'car': 6, 'bus': 3, 'bike': 10, 'cycle': 4, 'truck': 8}

上面的这个例子是一个普通的字典合并例子，使用循环，python 有一些内置的特性，我们可以利用它而不用循环来合并字典。

字典合并运算符

使用合并运算符（|）很容易进行字典合并，它可以从 python 3.9 获得

mydict1 = { "car": 5, "bus": 3, "bike": 10 } mydict2 = { "cycle": 4, "truck": 8, "car": 6 } print(mydict1 | mydict2) Output: {'car': 6, 'bus': 3, 'bike': 10, 'cycle': 4, 'truck': 8}

使用 ** 运算符合并字典

该运算符用于打包和解包参数，我们可以使用解包运算符合并两个字典

mydict1 = { "car": 5, "bus": 3, "bike": 10 } mydict2 = { "cycle": 4, "truck": 8, "car": 6 } print({**mydict1, **mydict2}) Output: {'car': 6, 'bus': 3, 'bike': 10, 'cycle': 4, 'truck': 8}

使用 update() 方法合并字典

使用解包运算符 (**) 和合并运算符 (|) 仅返回值，因为此处使用更新方法会将值更新为目标变量。

探索其他相关文章

如何检查字典中是否存在键python
一旦暂停，max24816 将无法发表评论或发布帖子，直到他们的暂停被解除。

一旦解除暂停，max24816 将能够再次发表评论和发布帖子。

一旦取消发布，max24816 的所有帖子都将隐藏起来，只有他们自己可以访问。

如果 max24816 没有被暂停，他们仍然可以从他们的仪表板重新发布他们的帖子。

笔记：

感谢您维护 DEV 社区的安全。 以下是标记 max24816 的方法：

降低 max24816 的所有帖子的可见度

报告其他不当行为

取消标记 max24816 将恢复其帖子的默认可见性。



原文:

   

Python Dictionary is a data structure that used to store elements in key-value pairs. Unlike python list values in the dictionary are retrieved or stored using the key. Dictionary is declared using the curly braces {} and the key value pair separated by : colon.

combine two dictionaries using python for loop 

mydict1 = { "car": 5, "bus": 3, "bike": 10
} mydict2 = { "cycle": 4, "truck": 8, "car": 6
} for key in mydict2: mydict1[key] = mydict2[key] print("mydict1:", mydict1) Output:
mydict1: {'car': 6, 'bus': 3, 'bike': 10, 'cycle': 4, 'truck': 8}

key car exists in both variable, we doing the merge operation if the key is not in the first mydict1 variable, it will add a new key value pair, if the key already exist in the dict it will update the value of the key.

This above example is a normal dictionaries merge example using loop, python have some inbuild features we can make use of it without doing for loop to combine dictionaries.

Dictionary Merge Operator

Using merge operator(|) it is easy to do the dictionary merge, its available from python 3.9

mydict1 = { "car": 5, "bus": 3, "bike": 10
} mydict2 = { "cycle": 4, "truck": 8, "car": 6
} print(mydict1 | mydict2) Output:
{'car': 6, 'bus': 3, 'bike': 10, 'cycle': 4, 'truck': 8}

Merging Dictionaries Using ** Operator

This operator is used to pack and unpacking arguments, we can use the unpacking operator to merge two dictionaries

mydict1 = { "car": 5, "bus": 3, "bike": 10
} mydict2 = { "cycle": 4, "truck": 8, "car": 6
} print({**mydict1, **mydict2}) Output:
{'car': 6, 'bus': 3, 'bike': 10, 'cycle': 4, 'truck': 8}

Merging Dictionaries Using the update() Method

Python have a inbuild method called update which is used to merge or add one dictionary to another

mydict1 = { "car": 5, "bus": 3, "bike": 10
} mydict2 = { "cycle": 4, "truck": 8, "car": 6
} mydict1.update(mydict2)
print(mydict1) Output:
{'car': 6, 'bus': 3, 'bike': 10, 'cycle': 4, 'truck': 8}

Using unpacking operator(**) and merge operator(|) only return the value where as here using the update method will update the values to the targeted variable.

Explore Other Related Articles

How to check if a key exists in a dictionary python
Python try exception tutorial
Python classes and objects tutorial
Python Recursion Function Tutorial
Python Lambda Function Tutorial
Python If, If-Else Statements Tutorial

Francesco Ciulla - Feb 12

TextEditingController emailController = TextEditingController(); TextEditingController mobileController = TextEditingController(); TextEditingController addressController = TextEditingController(); @override Widget build(BuildContext context) { return Scaffold( appBar: AppBar( title: Text('Registration Page'), ), //for the form to be in center body: Center( child: Container( padding: const EdgeInsets.all(20), child: ListView( shrinkWrap: true, children: [ const Text('Name'), const SizedBox( height: 5, ), TextField( controller: nameController, decoration: const InputDecoration( border: OutlineInputBorder(), hintText: 'Enter Name', ), ), //some space between name and email const SizedBox( height: 10, ), const Text('Email'), const SizedBox( height: 5, ), TextField( controller: emailController, decoration: const InputDecoration( border: OutlineInputBorder(), hintText: 'Enter Email', ), ), //some space between email and mobile const SizedBox( height: 10, ), const Text('Mobile'), const SizedBox( height: 5, ), TextField( controller: mobileController, decoration: const InputDecoration( border: OutlineInputBorder(), hintText: 'Enter Mobile', ), ), const SizedBox( height: 10, ), const Text('Address'), const SizedBox( height: 5, ), TextField( controller: addressController, decoration: const InputDecoration( border: OutlineInputBorder(), hintText: 'Enter Address', ), ), SizedBox( height: 10, ), //create button for register ElevatedButton( onPressed: () { //we will trying to print input print(nameController.text); print(emailController.text); print(mobileController.text); print(addressController.text); }, child: Text( 'Register', style: TextStyle( fontSize: 30, ), ), ), ], ), ), ), ); } }

降低 realnamehidden1_61 的所有帖子的可见性

报告其他不当行为

原文:

   

main.dart

import 'package:flutter/material.dart';

void main() {
  runApp(const MyApp());
}

class MyApp extends StatelessWidget {
  const MyApp({super.key});

  // This widget is the root of your application.
  @override
  Widget build(BuildContext context) {
    return MaterialApp(
      title: 'Flutter Demo',
      theme: ThemeData(
        primarySwatch: Colors.blue,
      ),
      home: const RegisterPage(),
      debugShowCheckedModeBanner: false,
    );
  }
}

class RegisterPage extends StatefulWidget {
  const RegisterPage({super.key});

  @override
  State<RegisterPage> createState() => _RegisterPageState();
}

class _RegisterPageState extends State<RegisterPage> {
  //create the controllers
  TextEditingController nameController = TextEditingController();
  TextEditingController emailController = TextEditingController();
  TextEditingController mobileController = TextEditingController();
  TextEditingController addressController = TextEditingController();
  @override
  Widget build(BuildContext context) {
    return Scaffold(
      appBar: AppBar(
        title: Text('Registration Page'),
      ),
      //for the form to be in center
      body: Center(
        child: Container(
          padding: const EdgeInsets.all(20),
          child: ListView(
            shrinkWrap: true,
            children: [
              const Text('Name'),
              const SizedBox(
                height: 5,
              ),
              TextField(
                controller: nameController,
                decoration: const InputDecoration(
                  border: OutlineInputBorder(),
                  hintText: 'Enter Name',
                ),
              ),
              //some space between name and email
              const SizedBox(
                height: 10,
              ),
              const Text('Email'),
              const SizedBox(
                height: 5,
              ),
              TextField(
                controller: emailController,
                decoration: const InputDecoration(
                  border: OutlineInputBorder(),
                  hintText: 'Enter Email',
                ),
              ),
              //some space between email and mobile
              const SizedBox(
                height: 10,
              ),
              const Text('Mobile'),
              const SizedBox(
                height: 5,
              ),
              TextField(
                controller: mobileController,
                decoration: const InputDecoration(
                  border: OutlineInputBorder(),
                  hintText: 'Enter Mobile',
                ),
              ),
              const SizedBox(
                height: 10,
              ),
              const Text('Address'),
              const SizedBox(
                height: 5,
              ),
              TextField(
                controller: addressController,
                decoration: const InputDecoration(
                  border: OutlineInputBorder(),
                  hintText: 'Enter Address',
                ),
              ),
              SizedBox(
                height: 10,
              ),
              //create button for register
              ElevatedButton(
                onPressed: () {
                  //we will trying to print input
                  print(nameController.text);
                  print(emailController.text);
                  print(mobileController.text);
                  print(addressController.text);
                },
                child: Text(
                  'Register',
                  style: TextStyle(
                    fontSize: 30,
                  ),
                ),
              ),
            ],
          ),
        ),
      ),
    );
  }
}

Stanislav Ilin - Aug 10 '22

item.title: 释放灵活性和效率：如何利用 Sagemaker 管道参数化 | Unlocking Flexibility and Efficiency: How to Leverage Sagemaker Pipelines Parameterization
item.description:
发表于 3 月 5 日

您是否正在将Sagemaker Pipelines 用于您的机器学习工作流？ 否则，您将错过一个可以简化从构建到部署的整个过程的强大工具。 但即使您已经熟悉这项服务，您也可能不知道它的一个关键特性：参数化。 通过参数化，您可以自定义整个工作流程，使其更加灵活和动态。 在本文中，我们将深入探讨此功能，分享一些真实示例，并讨论优缺点。 我们甚至会提供一些解决方法来解决此服务的局限性。 所以拿起你的咖啡，让我们开始吧！

在深入探讨 Sagemaker Pipelines 参数化的优缺点之前，让我们快速了解一下它的工作原理。 这些参数可以在定义管道时指定，它们的值可以在执行时设置。 参数化允许用户创建灵活的管道，可以针对不同的用例进行定制。

此外，Sagemaker studio 提供了一个令人惊叹的 GUI，可以使用定义参数的任何其他值来执行管道。

一般来说，Sagemaker Modeling Pipeline支持4 种不同类型的参数：

  • 语法很简单：

     <parameter> = <parameter_type>( name="<parameter_name>", default_value=<default_value> )

    真实世界的例子

    下面的帖子对入门非常有帮助：

    显然，参数化是在自动化 ML 工作流中使用 Sagemaker Pipelines 的一个关键优势。

    有许多优点，例如：

    • 基于 GUI 的执行：通常，可以定义一次管道，然后顺利执行整个工作流程。 如果您与喜欢低代码解决方案的同事数据科学家一起工作，这是一个很大的好处。 然而，我们仍然可以使用Sagemaker SDK执行它

    • 快速原型制作：显然，它可以通过轻松修改管道组件而无需进行大量手动更改，从而实现更高效的实验和测试。

    • 协作：通过将 ML 工作流程划分为模块化、参数化的部分。 现在，它对于团队合作更加实用和高效。

    • 自动化：它通过启用脚本和代码来修改管道参数来促进自动化，从而实现完全自动化的端到端机器学习工作流程。

    清单不胜枚举。

    虽然参数化是 Sagemaker Pipelines 的一项有用功能，但它也有一些限制，可能使其在某些情况下难以使用。 以下是一些需要注意的常见限制：

    • 对动态或运行时参数的有限支持：Sagemaker Pipelines 仅支持在管道定义期间设置的静态参数。 不支持可在管道执行期间设置的运行时或动态参数。

    • 对嵌套参数的有限支持：Sagemaker Pipelines 不支持嵌套参数或分层参数，这在更复杂的管道用例中可能会受到限制。

    • 有限的参数验证：Sagemaker Pipelines 不提供广泛的参数验证功能，这使得在管道执行期间更难发现错误或问题。 例如，它可能不会自动验证输入数据的格式或类型，或确保参数在可接受的范围或限制内。

    • 配额限制：AWS 为管道中的最大参数数量设置了 200 个不可调整的配额限制。 这可能是大规模管道中的一个问题。

    此外， 官方文档还列出了一些其他限制：

    • 与其他 Sagemaker Python SDK 模块不 100% 兼容：例如，管道参数不能用于为框架处理器传递 image_uri，但可以与处理器一起使用。
    • 并非所有参数都可以参数化：请记住仔细阅读文档以了解某个参数是否可以是管道变量。

    例如， role可以参数化，而base_job_name不能在处理器API 中参数化，如下所示。

    • 并非所有内置的 Python 操作都可以应用于参数。
     # An example of what not to do my_string = "s3://{}/training".format(ParameterString(name="MyBucket", default_value="")) # Another example of what not to do int_param = str(ParameterInteger(name="MyBucket", default_value=1)) # Instead, if you want to convert the parameter to string type, do int_param.to_string()

    有用的解决方法

    虽然这些限制会使 Sagemaker Pipelines 中的参数化具有挑战性，但有一些解决方案可以克服其中的一些限制。 这里有一些例子：

    • 将 Lambda 函数用于动态参数：要解决静态参数的限制，您可以使用 Lambda 函数根据其他管道输入或外部数据动态确定参数值。 例如，您可以使用 Lambda 函数根据所有客户评论的平均星级来计算要包含在您的分析中的最低星级。 您可以在此上下文中使用 Lambda 步骤。 将ProperyFiles用于嵌套参数：如果您需要指定嵌套或分层参数，您可以使用 JsonGet 和 PropertyFile 编写要在管道内使用的 JSON 文件，如下面的代码片段所示：

       import sagemaker from sagemaker.workflow.properties import PropertyFile from sagemaker.workflow.steps import ProcessingStep from sagemaker.processing import FrameworkProcessor import json from sagemaker.workflow.functions import JsonGet from sagemaker.sklearn.estimator import SKLearn from sagemaker.workflow.pipeline_context import PipelineSession from sagemaker.workflow.pipeline import Pipeline from sagemaker.processing import ProcessingOutput pipeline_session = PipelineSession() pp_outputs = [ProcessingOutput(output_name="paths", source="/opt/ml/processing/output")] Paths_file = PropertyFile( name="NestedParameter", output_name="paths", path="nested_parameter.json", ) pp = FrameworkProcessor(role=sagemaker.get_execution_role(), instance_type="ml.t3.medium", instance_count=1, estimator_cls=SKLearn, framework_version = "0.23-1", sagemaker_session = pipeline_session) step_args = pp.run(code = "DoNothing.py", outputs= pp_outputs) step_process = ProcessingStep( name="Dummystep", step_args=step_args, property_files=[Paths_file]) train_path = JsonGet( step_name=step_process.name, property_file=Paths_file, json_path="paths.train.URI", ) test_path = JsonGet( step_name=step_process.name, property_file=Paths_file, json_path="paths.test.URI", )

      构建自定义验证脚本：要捕获管道参数的错误或问题，您可以构建自定义验证脚本，在管道运行之前检查参数值。 这有助于尽早发现错误并防止因参数无效而导致管道故障。

    • 精心设计：总而言之，你需要设计暴露的参数，以确保不超过配额值。 如果您需要进一步增加，您可以就您的案例联系支持人员。

    结论

    参数化是 Sagemaker Pipelines 中的一项有用功能，但它确实有一些限制，可能使其在某些情况下难以使用。 通过使用 Lambda 函数、PropertyFiles 和自定义验证脚本，您可以解决其中一些限制并创建更灵活的管道。 通过遵循参数化的最佳实践，您还可以确保您的管道组织良好且易于使用。 通过这些提示和技巧，您将能够充分利用 Sagemaker Pipelines 参数化并创建强大的机器学习工作流。

一旦被暂停，aws-builders 将无法发表评论或发布帖子，直到他们的暂停被取消。

取消暂停后，aws-builders 将能够再次发表评论和发布帖子。

一旦取消发布，aws-builders 的所有帖子都将隐藏起来，只有他们自己可以访问。

如果 aws-builders 没有被暂停，他们仍然可以从他们的仪表板重新发布他们的帖子。

笔记：

感谢您维护 DEV 社区的安全。 您可以通过以下方式标记 aws-builders：

降低 aws-builders 的所有帖子的可见度

报告其他不当行为

取消标记 aws-builders 将恢复其帖子的默认可见性。



原文:

omarkhater for AWS Community Builders

Posted on Mar 5

   

Are you using Sagemaker Pipelines for your machine learning workflows yet? If not, you're missing out on a powerful tool that simplifies the entire process from building to deployment. But even if you're already familiar with this service, you may not know about one of its key features: parameterization. With parameterization, you can customize the whole workflow, making it more flexible and dynamic. In this article, we'll take a deep dive into this feature, share some real-world examples, and discuss the pros and cons. We'll even offer some workarounds for addressing the limitations of this service. So grab your coffee and let's get started!

Overview of Sagemaker Pipelines Parameterization

Before we dive into the pros and cons of Sagemaker Pipelines parameterization, let's take a quick look at how it works. Sagemaker Pipelines allows users to specify input parameters using the Parameter class. These parameters can be specified when defining the pipeline, and their values can be set at execution. Parameterization allows users to create flexible pipelines that can be customized for different use cases.

In addition, Sagemaker studio provides a stunning GUI to execute the pipeline using any other values for the defined parameters.

Generally speaking, Sagemaker Modeling Pipeline supports 4 different type of parameters:

  • ParameterString – Representing a string parameter.
  • ParameterInteger – Representing an integer parameter.
  • ParameterFloat – Representing a float parameter.
  • ParameterBoolean – Representing a Boolean Python type

and the syntax is as simple as:

<parameter> = <parameter_type>( name="<parameter_name>", default_value=<default_value>
)

Real-world Examples

Amazon Sagemaker Example Notebooks provides several complete posts about Sagemaker pipelines parameterization. Posts below are really helpful to get started with:

Benefits of Sagemaker Pipelines Parameterization

Clearly, parameterization is a key advantage of using Sagemaker Pipelines in automating ML workflows.

There are numerous advantages such as:

  • GUI-based executions: Typically, one can define the Pipeline once then execute the whole work flow smoothly. This is a significant benefit if you work with a colleague data scientist who prefer low-code solutions. Yet, we still can execute it using the Sagemaker SDK

  • Rapid prototyping: Evidently, it enables more efficient experimentation and testing by allowing for easy modification of pipeline components without the need for extensive manual changes.

  • Colloboration: By divding the ML workflow into modular, parameterized parts. It is now more practical and efficient for team-work.

  • Automation: It facilitates automation by enabling the use of scripts and code to modify pipeline parameters, allowing for fully automated end-to-end machine learning workflows.

The list goes on and on.

Limitations of Sagemaker Pipelines Parameterization

While parameterization is a useful feature of Sagemaker Pipelines, it also has some limitations that can make it difficult to use in certain situations. Here are some common limitations to be aware of:

  • Limited support for dynamic or runtime parameters: Sagemaker Pipelines only supports static parameters that are set during pipeline definition. There is no support for runtime or dynamic parameters that can be set during pipeline execution.

  • Limited support for nested parameters: Sagemaker Pipelines does not support nested parameters or hierarchical parameters, which can be limiting in more complex pipeline use cases.

  • Limited parameter validation: Sagemaker Pipelines does not provide extensive parameter validation capabilities, which can make it harder to catch errors or issues during pipeline execution. For example, it may not automatically validate the format or type of the input data, or ensure that the parameters are within acceptable ranges or limits.

  • Quota limiations: AWS sets a non-adjustable quota limit of 200 for the maximum number of parameters in the pipeline. This might be a problem in large-scale pipelines.

In addition, the official documentation listed some other limitations:

  • Not 100% compatible with other Sagemaker Python SDK modules: For example, pipeline parameters can't be used to pass image_uri for Framework Processors but can be used with Processor.
  • Not all arguments can be parameterized: Remember to read the documentation carefully to see whether a certain parameter can be a Pipeline variable or not.

For example, the role can be parameterized while the base_job_name can not be parameterized in the Processor API as shown below.

  • Not all built-in Python operations can be applied to parameters. 
# An example of what not to do
my_string = "s3://{}/training".format(ParameterString(name="MyBucket", default_value="")) # Another example of what not to do
int_param = str(ParameterInteger(name="MyBucket", default_value=1)) # Instead, if you want to convert the parameter to string type, do
int_param.to_string()

Useful Workarounds

While these limitations can make parameterization in Sagemaker Pipelines challenging, there are solutions for overcoming some of them. Here are a few examples:

  • Using Lambda functions for dynamic parameters: To work around the limitation of static parameters, you can use a Lambda function to determine the parameter value dynamically based on other pipeline inputs or external data. For example, you could use a Lambda function to calculate the minimum star rating to include in your analysis based on the average star rating of all customer reviews. You can use Lambda step in this context. All different steps are summarized in this post

  • Using ProperyFiles for nested parameters: If you need to specify nested or hierarchical parameters, you can write a JSON file to be used within the Pipeline using both JsonGet and PropertyFile as shown in the code snippet below:

    import sagemaker
from sagemaker.workflow.properties import PropertyFile
from sagemaker.workflow.steps import ProcessingStep
from sagemaker.processing import FrameworkProcessor
import json
from sagemaker.workflow.functions import JsonGet
from sagemaker.sklearn.estimator import SKLearn
from sagemaker.workflow.pipeline_context import PipelineSession
from sagemaker.workflow.pipeline import Pipeline
from sagemaker.processing import ProcessingOutput pipeline_session = PipelineSession()
pp_outputs = [ProcessingOutput(output_name="paths", source="/opt/ml/processing/output")]
Paths_file = PropertyFile( name="NestedParameter", output_name="paths", path="nested_parameter.json",
) pp = FrameworkProcessor(role=sagemaker.get_execution_role(), instance_type="ml.t3.medium", instance_count=1, estimator_cls=SKLearn, framework_version = "0.23-1", sagemaker_session = pipeline_session)
step_args = pp.run(code = "DoNothing.py", outputs= pp_outputs)
step_process = ProcessingStep( name="Dummystep", step_args=step_args, property_files=[Paths_file]) train_path = JsonGet( step_name=step_process.name, property_file=Paths_file, json_path="paths.train.URI",
)
test_path = JsonGet( step_name=step_process.name, property_file=Paths_file, json_path="paths.test.URI",
)

    The JSON file could be something like this:

    {
"paths": { "train": { "URI": "s3://<path_to_train_data>" }, "test": { "URI": "s3://<path_to_test_data>" }
}
}

  • Building custom validation scripts: To catch errors or issues with your pipeline parameters, you can build custom validation scripts that check the parameter values before the pipeline runs. This can help catch errors early and prevent pipeline failures due to invalid parameters.

  • Careful design: All in all, you need to design the exposed parameters to ensure not passing the quota value. If you need further increase, you may contact the support about your case.

Conclusion

Parameterization is a useful feature in Sagemaker Pipelines, but it does have some limitations that can make it challenging to use in certain situations. By using Lambda functions, PropertyFiles, and custom validation scripts, you can work around some of these limitations and create more flexible pipelines. By following best practices for parameterization, you can also ensure that your pipelines are well-organized and easy to use. With these tips and tricks, you'll be able to make the most of Sagemaker Pipelines parameterization and create powerful machine learning workflows.

item.title: 我在现实生活中应用的 4 条工程原理 | 4 Engineering principles I've applied in real-life
item.description:
我的雇主几个月前宣布，任何工作 3 年或以上的员工都有权休假。 这个休假是 1 个月的全薪假期（您选择的月份）。 由于我还有很多假期，我和我的妻子决定去亚洲旅行 2 个月。 我们想去那里很久了。 经过一番思考，我们决定我们的目的地是泰国和菲律宾。

虽然这次旅行很棒，而且我们有很多经验，但这篇文章将侧重于另一方面。 在这篇文章中，我将逐步描述我作为软件工程师如何使用日常工作中的方法。 应用它们帮助我解决了现实生活中的问题。

最终，某些东西会在生产中中断

俗话说，最终你会遇到生产事故。 在我们的例子中，它宜早不宜迟。 在我们降落在泰国几个小时后，我们发现我们遇到了一个大问题。 我的信用卡和我妻子的信用卡都在我们着陆那天过期了。 这对我们来说是一个很大的惊喜。 在国内，您的信用卡提供商将在您的卡过期前至少 6 个月更换您的卡。 然而，由于我们现在在德国生活了将近 4 年，我们学到了一些新东西。 在这里，如果你想拥有一张新卡，你需要向你的银行索要一张新卡（经验教训！）。 好消息是我们还带了一些现金去度假。

那么我们做了什么？

我们首先估计问题有多大、影响是什么、解决方案有哪些选择以及我们需要以多快的速度应用它们。 这次讨论的结果非常有用。 我们同意，例如，如果我们使用 Airbnb 进行预订，我们可以节省我们身上的现金。 我们还发现我们的现金足够暂时使用，这样我们就可以开始我们的假期了。

列出我们问题的可能解决方案使我们能够：

  • 了解问题的范围。

  • 了解可能的解决方案的复杂性。

  • 选择最适合我们需求的解决方案。

复古&动作单品

当我们计划我们的旅行时，我和我的妻子决定我们将为自己预订 5 天的 Airbnb。 这将使我们有足够的时间来应对时差，享受这座城市。 最后，虽然时差确实伴随了我们3-4天，但我们觉得我们可以停留更短的时间。 此外，我们的 Airbnb 还可以，但不是很好，而且有点太贵了。

那么我们做了什么？

正如标题所暗示的，我们有一个简短的复古。 我们谈论了我们在这里做得好的地方，以及我们做得不好的地方。 我们一致认为盲目行动不是一个好主意。 此外，如果我们不喜欢我们要去的目的地怎么办？ 为什么要长期承诺？

这次复古的结果是我们在整个旅行中使用的行动项目。 我们不会预订超过 2 晚的任何地方。 如果我们喜欢这个地方，我们可以稍后延长住宿时间。 这让我们可以选择更改酒店，如果我们不喜欢它，或者如果我们觉得我们想继续前往下一个目的地。

在旅行的第一周进行的 10-15 分钟的讨论帮助我们提前计划并避免了以后的挫败感。 这就是我所说的好投资！

总是有人检查你的工作

在我们旅行的某个时刻，我们不得不预订国内航班。 我为我们找到了航班，虽然不是理想的日期，但考虑到我们当前的位置，那是离我们最近的一次。 我们预订了航班，在我们的位置等了 3 天，然后乘船 6 小时。 所有这一切只是为了发现我犯了一个错误，然后我预订了一个月后的航班。

发生这种情况是有原因的，但最终结果是一样的。 我们到达机场时没有航班，也没有当地货币（第二个错误）。

那么我们学到了什么？

与您的代码一样，在合并之前您要求进行审查。 我们也这样做了。 对于任何预订（酒店、航班、巴士等），对方确认所有详细信息均正确无误。 您会惊讶于我们在旅途中彼此的预订中发现了多少小问题。

需求变更

当我们计划旅行时，我们想在泰国停留 1 个月，在菲律宾停留 1 个月。 然而，在泰国南部逗留期间，我们发现天气太热了。 我的妻子不喜欢炎热的天气，因此我们寻找了一个凉爽的目的地。 经过一番讨论，我们同意缩短访问时间，并在旅途中安排第三个目的地。 这就是我们在越南的结局！

那么我们学到了什么？

没有什么是一成不变的。 您最初看起来很匹配的东西可能会被发现是不匹配的。 始终根据用户的需求对变化持开放态度。 我们本可以说“我们已经计划好了一切”并保持我们最初的计划。 然而，但改变了我们的计划，我们让自己度过了一个更适合我们的假期。

结论

将工程界的实践应用到我们现实生活中的问题中是非常有益的。 我们的许多问题已经在不同的环境中得到解决，我们可以从这些解决方案中学习以满足我们的需求。

想要连接？

我很高兴在Linkedin或Twitter上建立联系。 随时接近我！

学分

  • 照片由Marc St在Unsplash上拍摄


原文:

My employer announced a couple of months ago that any employee who works for 3 years or more is entitled to a sabbatical. This sabbatical is a fully paid 1-month vacation (month of your choice). As I also had tons of vacation days, my wife and I decided to go on a 2 months trip to Asia. We wanted to go there for a long time. After some thinking, we decided that our destinations would be Thailand & Philippines.

While the trip was great, and we had a lot of experiences this post will focus on the other side. In this post, I would describe step by step how I used methods from my day-to-day work as a software engineer. Applying them helped me to solve problems in real life.

Eventually, something will break in production

As the old say goes, eventually you would have a production incident. In our case, it was sooner rather than later. A couple of hours after we landed in Thailand we discovered that we had a huge issue. Both my credit card & my wife's expired on the day we landed. That was a big surprise for us. Back home, your credit card provider will replace your card at least 6 months before it's expired. Yet, as we now living in Germany for almost 4 years, we learned something new. Here, you need to ask your bank for a new card if you want to have one (lesson learned!). Good thing was that we also brought some cash with us to the vacation.

So what did we do?

we started by estimating how big the problem, what is the impact, what are the options for solutions and how fast we need to apply them. The outcome of this discussion was very useful. We agreed that if we use Airbnb for booking for example we can save the usage of the cash we have with us. We also discovered that we had enough cash for a while, and thus we can start our vacation.

List the possible solutions to our problem enabled us to:

  • Understand the scope of the problem.

  • Understand the complexity of the possible solutions.

  • Choose the solution(s) that fit our needs best.

Retro & action items

When we planned our trip, my wife & I decided that we will book ourselves an Airbnb for 5 days. This will give us enough time to handle the jetlag, and enjoy the city. In the end, even though our jetlag indeed stayed with us for 3-4 days, we felt that we could stay for a shorter time. Moreover, our Airbnb was Ok, but not great, and a bit too pricey.

So what did we do?

As the title suggests, we had a short retro. We talked about what we did good, and what we did badly here. We agreed that going blindly is not a great idea. Moreover, what if we don't like the destination we're going to? Why should we commit for a long period?

The result of this retro was an action item that we used throughout our trip. We don't book any place for more than 2 nights. We can extend our staying later on if we like the place. This gave us the option to change the hotel if we didn't like it, or if we felt that we want to continue to our next destination.

This 10-15min discussion on the 1st week of the trip helped us plan ahead and avoid frustration later on. That's what I'm calling a good investment!

Always have someone checking your work

At some point in our trip, we had to book an internal flight. I've found us a flight, it wasn't on the ideal date, but it was the closest we had considering our current location. We booked the flight, we waited in our location for 3 days, and we went on a 6 hours boat ride. All that just to discover that I made a mistake and I booked the flight for a month after.

There are reasons why that happened, but the end result is the same. We arrived at the airport with no flight and no local money (mistake number two).

So what did we learn?

Like with your code, before merging you're asking for a review. We did the same. For any booking (hotel, flight, bus, etc) the other person validated that all details are correct. You would be surprised how many small issues we found in each other's bookings over the trip.

Requirements change

When we planned our trip, we wanted to stay for 1 month in Thailand, and 1 month in the Philippines. Yet, during our stay in the south of Thailand, we discovered that the weather was too hot. My wife doesn't like the hot weather, and thus we looked for a cooler destination. After some discussions, we agreed to cut our visit short and have a 3rd destination on our trip. That's how we ended up in Vietnam!

So what did we learn?

Nothing is set in stone. What you originally appeared as a good match can be discovered as a bad match. Always be open to changes based on what the user needs. We could have said "we already planned everything" and stay to our original plan. Yet, but changing our plan we enabled ourselves a vacation that was a better fit for us.

Conclusion

Applying practices from the engineering world to our real-life problems can be very beneficial. Many of our problems have already been solved in a different context, and we can learn from those solutions for our needs.

Want to connect?

I’m happy to connect on Linkedin or Twitter. Feel free to approach me!

Credits

  • Photo by Marc St on Unsplash

item.title: 你应该知道的独特 Git 命令！ | Unique Git Commands You Should Know!
item.description:
如果您了解 Git 并且有兴趣学习一些独特的 Git 命令，这些命令将使您的日常生活更轻松，那么本文适合您。 开始吧！

让我们分解一下：

因此， git commit -am命令使我们能够使用内联提交消息将所有更改一次添加并提交到存储库。 简单的！

每个条目都包含一个提交散列以及整个提交消息。

从技术上讲，这绝对没问题。 但在视觉上，如果我们设法将每个条目限制在一行中，它看起来会好很多。 你猜怎么着， --oneline标志就是这么做的！ 看看下面的截图：

好多了，不是吗？

假设你弄乱了一个分支的名字，你想重命名它。 你可以做到吗？

是的！ 将-m标志附加到git branch命令可以帮助您重命名分支。 在我们的例子中，我们将git-demo重命名为feature 。

通常需要执行两个步骤来签出新分支：

  • 使用git branch <branch_name>创建一个新分支。

  • 使用git switch <branch_name>切换到新创建的分支。

好消息！ 您可以使用git switch -c命令一步执行此操作。

简单，但有效！

结论

谁不想提高生产力？ 使用这些 Git 命令肯定会为您提供这些！ 使用它们并享受承诺！

一旦暂停，sriparno08 将无法发表评论或发布帖子，直到他们的暂停被解除。

解除暂停后，sriparno08 将能够再次发表评论和发布帖子。

一旦取消发布，sriparno08 的所有帖子都将隐藏起来，只有他们自己可以访问。

如果 sriparno08 没有被暂停，他们仍然可以从他们的仪表板重新发布他们的帖子。

原文:

Git is a free and open-source distributed version control system designed to handle everything from small to very large projects with speed and efficiency.

If you know Git and are interested in learning some unique Git commands that will make your day-to-day life easier, then this article is for you. Let's start!

git commit -am

The git commit -am command is a combination of the command git commit and the flags -a and -m. Let's break it down:

The git commit command commits all staged changes to the repository and opens up the default code editor for us to write a commit message.

The -a flag helps us add and commit all changes to the repository at once.

The -m flag allows us to write an inline commit message, bypassing the intermediate step of opening up the default code editor.

Hence, the git commit -am command enables us to add and commit all the changes to the repository at once with an inline commit message. Simple!

git log --oneline

The git log command displays the commit log of a repository. Each entry contains a commit hash along with the entire commit message.

Technically, it is absolutely fine. But visually, it would look a lot nicer if we somehow managed to restrict each entry to a single line. Guess what, the --oneline flag does exactly that! Take a look at the below screenshot:

Much better, isn't it?

git branch -m

The git branch command lists all the existing branches in a repository.

Suppose you have messed up the name of a branch and you want to rename it. Can you do it?

Yes! Appending the -m flag to the git branch command helps you rename a branch. In our case, we renamed git-demo to feature.

git switch -c

There are generally two steps that you need to follow to checkout a new branch:

  • Use git branch <branch_name> to create a new branch.

  • Use git switch <branch_name> to switch to the newly created branch.

Good news! You can perform this operation in one step using the git switch -c command.

git commit --amend

The --amend flag helps you to rewrite the commit message for the last commit.

Simple, but effective!

Conclusion

Who doesn't want a productivity boost? Using these Git commands will surely provide you with that! Use them and enjoy committing!

Vincent A. Cicirello - Jan 17

item.title: 伦敦廉价航班 | London Cheap flights
item.description:
廉价航班

发表于 3 月 5 日

寻找飞往伦敦的廉价航班可能有点困难，但您可以使用一些策略来省钱。 以下提示可帮助您找到飞往伦敦的实惠航班：

灵活安排您的旅行日期：如果您能够在工作日而不是周末旅行，或者如果您可以灵活安排旅行的确切日期，您可能会找到更便宜的航班。

使用价格比较网站：Skyscanner、Kayak 和 Expedia 等网站允许您同时搜索多家航空公司的航班，因此您可以比较价格并找到最优惠的价格。

提前预订：通常，提前几周或几个月预订航班可以帮助您找到更便宜的票价。 最后一刻的预订往往更贵。

寻找廉价航空公司：瑞安航空、易捷航空和威兹航空等低成本航空公司通常提供从欧洲各城市飞往伦敦的廉价航班。

考虑其他机场：伦敦有多个机场，因此您可以飞往斯坦斯特德或卢顿等较小的机场，而不是希思罗机场或盖特威克机场，从而找到更便宜的航班。

订阅航空公司时事通讯：航空公司通常会向订户提供独家优惠和促销活动，因此注册他们的时事通讯可以帮助您找到飞往伦敦和其他目的地的廉价航班。

通过使用这些策略，您可以增加找到飞往伦敦的廉价航班的机会。

原文:

Cheap flights

Posted on Mar 5

Finding cheap flights to London can be a bit of a challenge, but there are a few strategies you can use to save money. Here are some tips to help you find affordable flights to London:

Be flexible with your travel dates: If you're able to travel on weekdays instead of weekends, or if you can be flexible with the exact dates of your trip, you may be able to find cheaper flights.

Use price comparison websites: Websites like Skyscanner, Kayak, and Expedia allow you to search for flights from multiple airlines at once, so you can compare prices and find the best deal.

Book in advance: Generally, booking your flight several weeks or months in advance can help you find cheaper fares. Last-minute bookings tend to be more expensive.

Look for budget airlines: Low-cost carriers like Ryanair, EasyJet, and Wizz Air often offer cheap flights to London from cities across Europe.

Consider alternative airports: London has several airports, so you may be able to find cheaper flights by flying into a smaller airport like Stansted or Luton, rather than Heathrow or Gatwick.

Sign up for airline newsletters: Airlines often offer exclusive deals and promotions to subscribers, so signing up for their newsletters can help you find cheap flights to London and other destinations.

By using these strategies, you can increase your chances of finding affordable flights to London.
